library("tidyverse")

mosquito_data <- read_csv("data/mosquito_subset_wrangled.csv")
#> Rows: 1991 Columns: 14
#> ── Column specification ────────────────────────────────────
#> Delimiter: ","
#> chr  (9): genus, species, sample_unit, sample_sex, sampl...
#> dbl  (3): sample_value, sample_lat_dd, sample_long_dd
#> date (2): sample_start_date, sample_end_date
#> 
#> ℹ Use `spec()` to retrieve the full column specification for this data.
#> ℹ Specify the column types or set `show_col_types = FALSE` to quiet this message.
abundance_per_location <- mosquito_data %>%
  group_by(sample_location) %>%
  summarise(
    mean_abundance = mean(sample_value, na.rm = TRUE),
    se_abundance = sd(sample_value, na.rm = TRUE) / sqrt(sum(!is.na(sample_value)))
  )ggplot(
  abundance_per_location,
  aes(x = sample_location, y = mean_abundance)
  ) +geom_col(fill = "darkturquoise") +
  geom_errorbar(aes(
    ymin = mean_abundance - se_abundance,
    ymax = mean_abundance + se_abundance
  ),
  width = 0.2) +
labs(
  title = "Mean Abundance of Culex pipiens Across Locations",
  x = "Sampling Location",
  y = "Mean Abundance"
)
abundance_plot_across_locations <- ggplot(
  abundance_per_location,
  aes(x = sample_location, y = mean_abundance)
  ) +
  geom_col(fill = "darkturquoise") +
  geom_errorbar(
    aes(
      ymin = mean_abundance - se_abundance,
      ymax = mean_abundance + se_abundance
    ),
    width = 0.2) +
  labs(
    title = "Mean Abundance of Culex pipiens Across Locations",
    x = "Sampling Location",
    y = "Mean Abundance"
  )

abundance_plot_across_locations
ggsave("daily_abundance_all.pdf", plot = daily_abundance_plot_all)

heading: 3.4.4 Using Faceting to Improve Clarity
text: One useful solution overcrowding faceting, feature ggplot2 splits single plot multiple smaller panels.panel displays subset data, allowing patterns compared side--side without overlapping elements. separating data smaller panels, can often reveal patterns might otherwise hidden within crowded visualisation.Faceting particularly useful :Comparing multiple species.Comparing sampling sites.Exploring patterns across environmental conditions.can apply faceting previous time-series visualisation separate sampling location panel, rather using different colours. directly addresses overlapping lines, improving overall clarity readability., add facet_wrap() existing code, tells R want generate several panels separated location:separating sampling location panel, patterns data become much clearer:Seasonal trends still evident across locations, now able see patterns vary locations easily.Differences abundance magnitude much clearer, locations consistently showing higher peaks, others reflecting relatively low abundance across time-series.Extreme peaks can clearly associated specific locations without overlapping lines hiding observations.Overall, faceting improves clarity reducing visual clutter allowing direct comparisons locations. makes easier interpret independent trends per location compare patterns across multiple locations.Optional refinement: current visualisation, panels share y-axis scale, good comparisons. However, scale also means extreme peaks visually dominant, locations smaller peaks look flat comparison.can add scales = "free_y" within facet_wrap() adjust y-axis scales across panels, allowing us visualise patterns per location greater detail:

  daily_abundance_all_locations,

) +

heading: 7.4 Data Wrangling Principles
text: Data wrangling process cleaning, transforming, organising raw data format suitable analysis.Data retrieved Hub Search ohvbd package often raw, missing values messy data formats. Understanding data wrangling principles help organise data usable formats make statistical analysis smoother easier.

heading: 7.4.1 Do’s and Don’ts of Data Wrangling
text: 1. Understand data first.: Explore data making changes. can look first rows dataset using head(), allows us better understand data, including checking column names data types.Don’t: Jump straight cleaning data without fully understanding formatted. Without understanding data, risk misinterpreting variables accidentally removing useful data.2. Save raw data.: Keep unchanged version raw dataset can access previous version something goes wrong, reproduce work, verify results.Don’t: Overwrite original data. wrangling data R, assign cleaned data new object: clean_data <- raw_data.3. Use clear & consistent naming.: Use informative column, model, object names know object explicitly. Clear naming makes R workflow easier others () understand.Don’t: Use unclear names names messy formatting. Try avoid spaces, special characters, specific abbreviations.4. Reformat data.: Convert data long format analysis, using pivot_longer(). format, row represents single observation, can make data easier filter analyse.Don’t: Use data wide format, values spread across multiple columns. can limit data wrangling processing, grouping across different years generating effective visualisations.5. Record .: Keep track progress recording changes made used approach. good practice note changes comments code.Don’t: Rely memory alone. easy forget analytical methods used, chose approach, order processed data . Keeping clear records workflow contributes better reproducibility.Frequent Mistake: R handle spaces object names. options alternative syntax, recommend using camelCase letters capitalised indicate new words (e.g. specificModelName), using underscore connect words (e.g. specific_model_name).using camelCase, remember R case sensitive - object named specificModelName, call specificmodelname, R show error:Error: object ‘specificmodelname’ found
